@chezVoila Yes, exactly like that, but, from my friends / twitter followers
I am gonna let this first wash dry and try making up for my sloppiness with an attempt at little detailed trees, but my hopes are low. Also all watercolors look like garbage at this stage.
Also I think I should probably use this enormous paper to draw way more things at the same size as my notebook, instead of trying to scale up my notebook drawings, but this was a fun experiment.
To cover large areas evenly without these whack sloppy bushstrokes I am gonna need a bigger brush. Like significantly, comically bigger. https://t.co/DFcPdSi8sE

Drips, so many drips. This is from being lazy, and I need to be more steady with my pressure. There is a timing thing too, need to move fast when the brush is full of water and slow when it is running out. Briefly convinced myself I liked the drips and encouraged them. https://t.co/v7uNRxQBdz
So watercolors at large scale presents a number of new and interesting problems as compared to notebook scale https://t.co/Q0GBVL0mBu
Fringe, you can't just make the alternate universe crazy and not explain it at all. Jeez.

Growing up one thing I always loved to do was unclog the shower, or clean roof gutters, a visceral reward from clearing a path to let things flow – working with data / computers gives me that feeling sometimes (which I think is why I still do it)

was having a conversation lately with @enjalot – he was talking about how there is an innately rewarding feeling that comes with sorting through a large amount of things for a small thing that is good, and finding it. That's an experience you can find a lot of ways, data is one.
10 years of processing data and it still feels like magic
